Name: compression_sufficiency
Title: Compression Sufficiency: A Rate-Distortion Framework for Minimalist Model Representations
Short Hypothesis: The minimal bit-cost required for a model to retain task-relevant information can be quantified using rate-distortion theory, providing a theoretical foundation for understanding compression sufficiency in neural networks.
Related Work: Existing work on model compression (e.g., Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2021) focuses on pruning and quantization but lacks a unified framework to quantify the minimal bit-cost required for task-relevant sufficiency. While rate-distortion theory has been applied to model compression, it has not been explicitly used to derive minimal sufficient representations. Our proposal bridges this gap by framing compression sufficiency as a rate-distortion problem, distinct from prior work that primarily focuses on empirical compression gains or generalization error reduction.
Abstract: Model compression techniques such as pruning and quantization are widely used to reduce the computational and memory footprint of neural networks. However, the fundamental question of how much compression is sufficient to retain task-relevant information remains unanswered. In this work, we propose a rate-distortion framework to quantify the minimal bit-cost required for a compressed model to remain task-sufficient. We derive theoretical bounds on the compression sufficiency for various tasks and empirically validate these bounds on benchmark datasets. Our experiments demonstrate that our framework can guide compression strategies to achieve minimal sufficient representations without sacrificing task performance. This work provides a theoretical foundation for understanding compression limits and informs the design of efficient neural architectures.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Derive theoretical bounds on compression sufficiency for classification tasks using rate-distortion theory. Evaluate the bounds on MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets.
Experiment 2: Validate the minimal bit-cost required for compression sufficiency on ImageNet using ResNet-50. Compare with state-of-the-art pruning and quantization methods.
Experiment 3: Test the framework on a language modeling task using GPT-2, measuring the trade-off between bit-cost and task performance (e.g., perplexity).
Evaluation Metrics: Task accuracy, bits-per-weight, compression ratio, and distortion (task-specific loss).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical bounds may be difficult to derive for complex tasks or architectures, limiting applicability.
Empirical validation on large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet) may require significant computational resources.
The framework assumes task-specific distortion metrics, which may not generalize across all tasks.
The proposed bounds may not account for practical hardware constraints, such as quantization granularity.
